summary: The Gaming Center UI (WPF, net10.0-windows) has NO DI container: App.xaml.cs just does `new MainWindow().Show()`, MainWindow news up the four views directly, and each view instantiates its own stub service in a field initializer (`new LocalFanService()`, `new LocalPowerService()`, `new LocalRgbService()`). Those stubs (Services/StubServices.cs) hold in-memory state and honestly report writes as Blocked when the GAMINGCENTER_ALLOW_EC_WRITES env gate is off, behind the interfaces + ControlResult/WriteState contract in Services/ControlServices.cs. A fully-built real backend exists (Core.Windows/WmiEcBackend + Core/Platforms SafeEcWriter/WriteGate/EcWriteAllowlist) but is wired ONLY into the ASP.NET Server (GamingCenter.Server/Program.cs via Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection) — the UI never calls it or the HTTP API. There is zero reactive/MVVM/INotifyPropertyChanged/data-binding: state lives imperatively in code-behind fields and WPF control properties, and updates are pushed via plain C# events. The only injection is MainWindow constructing one SensorPump and passing it to DashboardView and FanView; telemetry flows through SensorPump.Tick (an event Action<Telemetry?>) on a 1 Hz DispatcherTimer.
recommendation: Introduce the standard at three seams, in this order. (1) A composition root in App.OnStartup: build one service registry (minimal Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection host or a hand-rolled container — the app already references it transitively) that selects Local* stubs vs future Core-backed implementations of IFanService/IPowerService/IRgbService once, and pass it (plus the single SensorPump) into MainWindow and the views. This kills the four scattered `new Local...()` field initializers and the static Toaster workaround. (2) A per-domain observable state store as the single source of truth (FanState, PowerState, RgbState, and a TelemetryStore). Wrap SensorPump's event Action<Telemetry?> as the telemetry store's push source (keep the 1 Hz DispatcherTimer / dispatcher affinity), and make each store expose INotifyPropertyChanged (idiomatic for WPF, lowest friction) or IObservable so views bind/subscribe instead of imperatively poking controls in OnTelemetry/OnLoaded. On writes, the store should re-read and reconcile so the UI truly mirrors live state rather than assuming success. (3) Keep the existing ControlResult / WriteState (Allowed/Executed/Verified/Error) contract as the standardized write-result vocabulary — do NOT reinvent it; it already matches Core's EcWriteResult and the API DTOs, so it is the clean bridge when the real WmiEcBackend/SafeEcWriter is swapped behind the same interfaces. Preserve Telemetry's all-nullable "n/a" semantics, the Debouncer live-apply-on-settle pattern, and MotionPrefs gating when building the reactive/animation layer. RGB stays Available=false until a HID IRgbBackend is implemented (only the interface exists today).

patterns.name: Service acquisition: no DI, views new-up stubs (SEAM #1 — composition root)
patterns.whatItIs: App.xaml.cs OnStartup only calls `new MainWindow().Show()` (plus a sensor-dump diagnostic branch). MainWindow.xaml.cs holds `new RgbView()` and `new PowerView()` as field initializers and constructs `new DashboardView(_pump)`/`new FanView(_pump)`. Each view then hard-codes its service: FanView `new LocalFanService()`, DashboardView `new LocalFanService()`, PowerView `new LocalPowerService()`, RgbView `new LocalRgbService()`. No IServiceProvider/IServiceCollection anywhere in the UI (grep-confirmed; those APIs appear only in Server/Program.cs). Because there is no container, ToastHost uses a static `Toaster` hub and its own comment says: 'The app has no DI container (views new-up their own services).'
patterns.fitForUs: Primary seam to standardize. A single-user hardware app benefits from one composition root that picks Local* stubs vs Core-backed impls once, at startup, instead of four scattered `new Local...()` calls. Today swapping to real hardware means editing every view.
patterns.tradeoffs: Minimal-DI (MS.Extensions.DependencyInjection Host) or a hand-rolled container passed App->MainWindow->views would centralize this. Two views (RgbView/PowerView) are instantiated by XAML-style parameterless ctors, so full ctor injection needs a locator/service field or converting all four to explicit construction (already done for the pump). The pump-sharing in MainWindow is the existing precedent for manual injection.

patterns.name: Write contract: ControlResult / WriteState already mirrors the real backend (SEAM #2 — writes)
patterns.whatItIs: Services/ControlServices.cs defines `ControlResult(bool Allowed, bool Executed, bool Verified, string? Error)` with factories Ok()/Blocked()/Failed() and a `State` property mapping to `enum WriteState { Idle, Pending, Verified, Failed, Blocked }`. Every write is: view calls `await _service.SetXAsync(...)`, gets a ControlResult, calls `Toaster.Show(result.State, label, result.Error)`. Interfaces: IFanService (GetMode/SetMode string auto|boost|custom|L1..L5, GetCurve/SetCurve of FanPoint(TempC,Pwm 0..140), WritesEnabled); IPowerService (GetAsync->PowerState(PerformanceMode Gaming/High/Balanced/Saving, PowerLimits Pl1/Pl2/Pl4 watts), SetModeAsync, SetLimitsAsync, WritesEnabled); IRgbService (GetStateAsync->RgbState?, ApplyAsync(RgbEffect), Available). Critically, ControlResult's Allowed/Executed/Verified is a 1:1 shape match for Core's EcWriteResult (Allowed/Executed/Verified/Error) from SafeEcWriter, and for the API's FanCurveWriteResultDto/PowerWriteResultDto.
patterns.fitForUs: Strong fit. For an app that must mirror live system state, the Allowed/Executed/Verified/Error vocabulary is exactly the honesty contract needed, and it already spans stub, Core backend, and HTTP DTO. Keep it as the write-side standard; do not reinvent.
patterns.tradeoffs: ControlResult->EcWriteResult mapping is by-convention, not code — a real backend must translate SafeEcWriter's per-register EcWriteResult into one per-action ControlResult (FanController already aggregates multiple register writes into one DTO). Writes are one-shot request/response, not observable; a reactive store would also re-read/verify state after a write to keep the mirror truthful.

patterns.name: Telemetry: SensorPump event push, manually shared (SEAM #3 — read/observe)
patterns.whatItIs: SensorPump (Hardware/SensorPump.cs) owns one HardwareMonitor (LibreHardwareMonitor, ring-0) and a 1 Hz DispatcherTimer. Construction is cheap; the monitor opens lazily on first Start(). Each tick calls `_monitor.GetTelemetry()` and raises `public event Action<Telemetry?> Tick` on the UI thread (null = sensors unavailable). Telemetry (Hardware/Telemetry.cs) is an immutable record of all-nullable doubles (CPU/GPU/RAM/swap/VRAM). MainWindow creates exactly one pump and passes it to DashboardView and FanView (ctor injection); views subscribe in Loaded (`_pump.Tick += OnTelemetry`), unsubscribe in Unloaded, then imperatively poke WPF controls (CpuGauge.Load = t.CpuLoadTotal ?? 0, etc). Disk/network are sampled separately inside DashboardView (DiskMeter/NetworkMeter, every Nth tick, overlap-guarded).
patterns.fitForUs: Natural home for an IObservable<Telemetry?> (or an INotifyPropertyChanged snapshot views bind to). The pump already has the single-owner, single-source-of-truth shape a reactive store wants; the manual `event Action` is a hand-rolled observable.
patterns.tradeoffs: Must stay on the UI/dispatcher thread (WPF affinity) — any Rx layer needs ObserveOn(Dispatcher) or to preserve the DispatcherTimer. The all-nullable Telemetry ('null means this machine doesn't expose it') is deliberate and must be preserved so the UI keeps saying 'n/a' rather than showing 0.

patterns.name: State ownership is fragmented across three layers (SEAM #4 — single source of truth)
patterns.whatItIs: No state store. Domain state lives inside stub services (_mode, _curve, _limits, _state). UI/derived state lives directly on WPF controls (RadioButton.IsChecked, Slider.Value, gauge props, TextBlock.Text). Transient/control-flow flags live in code-behind (_loading, _initialized, _started, _advancedDirty, _diskBusy, _tick). Views read initial state once in Loaded via `await _service.GetXAsync()` and never reconcile — PowerView reads GetAsync() once; FanView reads GetMode/GetCurve once. Nothing re-reads after a write except by local optimistic assumption (FanView calls SelectMode('custom') after a verified curve write).
patterns.fitForUs: Core gap for 'mirror live system state'. State is duplicated between stub, controls, and flags, so there is no single observable truth to render and no mechanism to reflect external/hardware changes back into the UI. A per-domain observable state store (fan/power/rgb/telemetry) is the standardization target.
patterns.tradeoffs: A store means deciding write-then-reread vs optimistic-update semantics, and untangling the many InitializeComponent-ordering guards (ValueChanged/Checked fire during XAML init before sibling elements exist — PowerView.OnLimitChanged, RgbView.OnEffectChecked). Those guards exist because there is no load/bind lifecycle; a proper VM/binding layer would replace them but must handle the same init-order hazard.

patterns.name: Real backend fully built but UI-disconnected; only the Server consumes it
patterns.whatItIs: Core.Windows/WmiEcBackend implements both IEcBackend (ReadSnapshotAsync, InterpretFanModeAsync, ReadPowerProfileAsync) and IEcWriter (WriteAsync) over WMI root\WMI AcpiTest_MULong GetSetULong (read flag 2^40|addr; write (value<<16)|flag|addr; PNP0C14 instance preferred). SafeEcWriter (Core/Platforms) orchestrates the safe pipeline: gate check -> allowlist check -> before-snapshot -> write -> read-back verify -> rollback-on-mismatch -> audit, returning EcWriteResult. WriteGate is env-driven (GAMINGCENTER_ALLOW_EC_WRITES, default deny; static Disabled). EcWriteAllowlist whitelists (address,value) pairs (fan control 1873/0x751; PWM 1859-1863; speculative PL1/PL2/PL4 1919-1921 and Tau 1857 left wide-open 0..255 pending hardware validation). Server/Program.cs wires all of this via AddSingleton and exposes it through FanController/PowerController; a JsonlWriteAuditLog and loopback-only middleware round it out. The UI's .csproj references GamingCenter.Api (transitively pulling Core), but grep confirms NO UI code instantiates WmiEcBackend/SafeEcWriter, uses HttpClient, or hits 127.0.0.1:5055 — the only reference is a comment in StubServices.cs. The UI uses Core only for shared RGB value types (RgbState/RgbEffect/RgbColor).
patterns.fitForUs: The backend + Server prove out the exact contract the UI stubs imitate, so a Core-backed IFanService/IPowerService/IRgbService can wrap SafeEcWriter/WmiEcBackend (or call the local HTTP API) and drop in behind the same interfaces. WriteGate's env flag is the same GAMINGCENTER_ALLOW_EC_WRITES the UI's WriteGateInfo reads, so gating stays consistent.
patterns.tradeoffs: Two wiring paths (in-proc Core reference vs HTTP to the loopback Server) with different elevation/lifetime implications — WmiEcBackend needs admin/ring-0; the UI already requires elevation for LibreHardwareMonitor, so in-proc is plausible. No IRgbBackend implementation exists at all (only the interface in Core/Rgb/IRgbBackend.cs), so RGB stays Available=false until a HID backend is written.

patterns.name: Existing push primitives and reduced-motion helper (what a standard would build on)
patterns.whatItIs: Reactive-ish push today is plain C# events: SensorPump.Tick (Action<Telemetry?>), HsvPicker.ColorChanged and FanCurveEditor.CurveChanged (EventHandler). Debouncer (Controls/Debouncer.cs) coalesces rapid edits (slider/curve drag) into one trailing action via a DispatcherTimer — used for live-apply-on-settle writes. Toaster/ToastHost is a static singleton feedback hub keyed on WriteState. MotionPrefs (Controls/MotionPrefs.cs) is a static reduced-motion switch: `ReducedMotion` returns `s_override ?? !SystemParameters.ClientAreaAnimation`, with `SetOverride(bool?)` to force it; every animation in ToastHost gates on MotionPrefs.ReducedMotion. There is no INotifyPropertyChanged, no ObservableCollection, no IObservable/Rx, no ViewModel, and no XAML data binding (only TemplateBinding inside Theme/Controls.xaml style templates).
patterns.fitForUs: The team already reaches for singletons, event-push, debounced live-apply, and a global motion switch — a standardized reactive layer should absorb these idioms (make MotionPrefs a queryable static the animation layer respects, keep Debouncer as the write-coalescing primitive, generalize Toaster+WriteState as the write-feedback standard) rather than replace them.
patterns.tradeoffs: Static singletons (Toaster, MotionPrefs) are convenient without DI but complicate testing and multi-window scenarios; if a container is introduced they should become injected services. MotionPrefs reads the OS setting once (no live-change subscription).
